Loading WMS-Rot…

WMS-Rot Suite

WMS-Rot: Rotor Spectrum Simulator

Unified branding for simulation, preparation, wavefunction inspection, and fitting across the WMS-Rot toolchain.

A Note on Privacy

We strongly believe in collaboration and open science. Privacy and security are central to how this tool is designed. For us, the web is an open platform that, without relying on system-dependent compiled software, better reflects those values through accessibility, transparency, and portability.

Although WMS-Rot is presented as a website, all code is executed locally on the user's own machine within the user's own web browser. No data is collected, uploaded, or processed by a remote background server, because there is no background server running the calculations. Everything runs locally in the browser. WMS-Rot can also be saved as a web app on the computer, and it can continue to run even after the network connection is interrupted.

Follow the workflow below to prepare or import the input, simulate the spectrum in WMS-Rot, and then move directly into fitting or wavefunction inspection.

1

Core Calculation

Simulate the Rotational Spectrum

Upload an existing input file or enter the parameters manually. This is the main working area of the page.

Input File

Load an existing WMS-Rot input

Prepare a WMS-Rot input

Generate the template with WMS-Prep

WMS-Prep extracts final DPCS3 geometry, starting rotational constants, quartic distortion constants, dipole components, and DVib corrections from multi-step Gaussian workflows.

Open WMS-Prep

Or build the input directly below. The form below and the export button already let you create a valid WMS-Rot .txt input file.

Model equations and selection rules
Physical Conditions and Symmetry

If not specified, the rotational partition function is calculated automatically.

Standard Parameters

Rotational Constants (MHz)

DVib (A/B/C) are anharmonic corrections computed as the difference between rotational constants at the |0> level and the equilibrium constants.

Dipole Moment (Debye)

A spherical rotor has zero permanent dipole; this value is only a placeholder.
Additional Hamiltonian Contributions

Choose the convention that matches the constants entered below. Need to reorient the coordinates first? Open WMS-Prep to rewrite coordinates consistently and generate the WMS-Rot input template automatically.

Choose the Watson reduction corresponding to the centrifugal-distortion constants entered below.

Quartic Centrifugal Distortion (kHz)

Make sure the values of centrifugal distortions are compatible with the selected representation.

Sextic Centrifugal Distortion (kHz)

Hyperfine Quadrupole (SPCAT-like)

Nucleus 1

Nucleus 2 (optional)

Up to two quadrupolar nuclei are supported. Use only the first block for single-nucleus cases.

Nuclear Statistics
Levels and Transition Options

Frequency Window

Simulation range

→

Values are treated as MHz. For backward compatibility, if Max ≤ 1000 it is interpreted as GHz.

Active Components

Thresholds

How J-range estimation works (rigid analytic model)

The estimator uses a rigid symmetric-top approximation and ignores centrifugal distortion, quadrupole coupling, and intensities. Frequency-window bounds are normalized to MHz before the estimate.

Rigid energies (MHz):

Prolate: E(J,K) = B J(J+1) + (A-B)K²
Oblate: E(J,K) = B J(J+1) + (C-B)K²

Main transition used:

For ΔJ = +1, ΔK = 0, the rigid estimate is
ν ≈ 2B(J+1).

Symmetric-top edge envelope (prolate/oblate):

For ΔJ = +1, ΔK = ±1, the code uses only the two analytic edge values
ν ≈ |2B(J+1) + γ m|, with γ = A-B (prolate) or γ = C-B (oblate), and m ∈ {-2J+1, 2J+1}.

Any J whose estimated transition frequency overlaps the selected window contributes to the final range. For asymmetric rotors, both prolate and oblate estimates are computed and merged: J_min = min(...), J_max = max(...).

For asymmetric rotors, the estimate combines prolate and oblate symmetric-top approximations in the selected frequency window, then runs the final asymmetric-top diagonalization with the inferred J range.

Calculated Output

Simulated Spectrum

Inspect the simulated branches here, then continue with the post-processing section for fitting, wavefunction analysis, or result export.

Visualization Options
2

Post-Processing

Fit or interpret the calculated spectrum

Once a spectrum has been calculated, continue with fitting, wavefunction inspection, or result export.

Fit

WMS-FitRot

Send the current calculated spectrum and input setup to WMS-FitRot, or open it directly and restore a saved Fit Status LOG.

Interpret

WMS-Wavefunctions

Inspect calculated wavefunctions to obtain a more qualitative and interpretative view of the states.

Export

Spectra Files

Download the calculated spectrum, energies, and export files for external analysis.

Calculate a spectrum in Core Calculation to enable post-processing tools.
